title: Employment Relations Act 1999
commencementNotes: Multiple commencement orders: SI 1999/2509 (25 Sep 1999), SI 1999/2830 (25 Oct 1999 and 15 Dec 1999), SI 2000/875 (24 Apr 2000), SI 2000/1338 (6 Jun 2000). Not all provisions commenced simultaneously.

whitePaper.title: Fairness at Work
whitePaper.significance: The White Paper set out the Blair government's programme for trade union recognition, the right to be accompanied, family leave reforms, and blacklist protections. The Act implements Chapter 4 (recognition), Chapter 5 (industrial action reform), and Chapter 6 (individual employment rights) of the White Paper.

parliamentaryOrigin.sponsoringMinister: Stephen Byers MP, Secretary of State for Trade and Industry

secondReadingHC.speaker: Stephen Byers MP, Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
secondReadingHC.summary: The Secretary of State set out the Bill's three principal objectives: implementing the statutory recognition procedure for trade unions, establishing the right to be accompanied at disciplinary and grievance hearings, and introducing family-friendly employment rights including parental leave and time off for dependants.

secondReadingHCOpposition.speaker: David Heathcoat-Amory MP, Shadow Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
secondReadingHCOpposition.summary: The Opposition expressed concerns about the impact of statutory recognition on small businesses and the balance between collective and individual employment rights.

committeeStageDates.summary: Detailed scrutiny of Clauses 1–6 (trade union recognition). Significant debate on the 21-worker threshold for Schedule A1 and the role of the CAC in determining bargaining units.

Scrutiny of Clauses 7–9 (family leave). Debate on the scope of parental leave entitlement and whether it should be paid or unpaid. Government confirmed the compromise position of 13 weeks unpaid leave.

Scrutiny of Clauses 10–15 (right to be accompanied). Significant debate on the scope of the companion's role and whether legal representatives should be included in s.10(3). Government resisted amendment to include lawyers.

reportStageHC.summary: Report Stage considered amendments to the recognition procedure (Schedule A1 ballot threshold) and the right to be accompanied. The 40% minimum turnout requirement for recognition ballots was debated and retained.

secondReadingHL.summary: Lords Second Reading. Peers raised concerns about the complexity of the statutory recognition procedure and the adequacy of protections for small employers.

lordsStages.committeeSummary: Lords Committee Stage saw significant amendments debated on Schedule A1, including proposals to raise the 21-worker threshold and to require a 50% membership threshold before CAC could declare recognition without a ballot.
lordsStages.reportAndThird: Lords Report and Third Reading completed June 1999. Government accepted some technical amendments but resisted substantive changes to the recognition procedure.

royalAssent.statement: The Employment Relations Act 1999 received Royal Assent on 27 July 1999.

sections.title: Collective bargaining: recognition
sections.summary: Inserts Schedule A1 into the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992, establishing the statutory procedure by which trade unions can obtain recognition from employers for collective bargaining purposes. Applies to employers with 21 or more workers.
sections.text:
(1) The Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 shall be amended as follows.

(2) After section 70 there shall be inserted—
"70A Recognition of trade unions
Schedule A1 shall have effect."

(3) Immediately before Schedule 1 there shall be inserted the Schedule set out in Schedule 1 to this Act.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 1, together with Schedule A1, creates the statutory recognition framework — the most significant trade union reform since the Trade Union Act 1984.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.70A (inserted by this section)
TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 (inserted by this section via ERA 1999 Schedule 1)
ERA 1996 s.104A (automatically unfair dismissal connected with recognition — inserted by ERA 1999 s.6)
Fairness at Work White Paper Cm 3968 Chapter 4

leadingCases.name: R (Kwik-Fit (GB) Ltd) v Central Arbitration Committee
leadingCases.principle: The CAC has broad discretion in determining the appropriate bargaining unit under Schedule A1 paragraph 19. The court will only intervene on judicial review where the CAC has acted irrationally or made an error of law.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the CAC's determination of the bargaining unit was a matter of industrial relations judgment, not a purely legal question. The court's role on judicial review was limited to examining whether the CAC had properly directed itself in law and had reached a decision that was not irrational. The CAC is not required to accept the union's proposed bargaining unit or the employer's preferred alternative.
leadingCases.significance: The first major Court of Appeal authority on the judicial review of CAC recognition decisions. Established the high threshold for challenging CAC bargaining unit determinations and confirmed the CAC's expertise in industrial relations matters.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Trade union recognition
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on voluntary and statutory recognition, including the employer's options when receiving a recognition request, the CAC process, and the obligations that flow from recognition.

commonErrors: Failing to identify that the statutory recognition threshold applies to employers with 21 or more workers, not 21 or more employees — the 'worker' definition is broader
Overlooking that voluntary recognition agreements remain valid and are not superseded by the statutory procedure
Failing to recognise that statutory recognition once granted can only be ended through the derecognition procedures in Schedule A1

sections.title: Detriment related to trade union membership
sections.summary: Amends TULRCA 1992 through Schedule 2, strengthening protections against detriment short of dismissal for trade union membership, activities, or use of trade union services. Extends protection to cover omissions as well as positive acts by the employer.
sections.text: Schedule 2 (which amends Part III of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992) shall have effect.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.146 (amended by ERA 1999 Schedule 2)
TULRCA 1992 s.147–150 (amended by ERA 1999 Schedule 2)
ERA 1999 Schedule 2
commonErrors: Assuming that detriment must involve a financial loss — detriment includes any disadvantage, including exclusion from meetings, denial of training opportunities, or unfavourable shift allocations
Failing to recognise that a deliberate omission (failure to act) can constitute detriment — TULRCA 1992 s.146(1) covers both acts and deliberate failures to act

Not understanding that the protection extends to participating in trade union activities 'at an appropriate time' — activities during working hours may be protected if undertaken with the employer's consent or during breaks

sections.title: Blacklists
sections.summary: Provides the Secretary of State with regulation-making power to prohibit the compilation, use, sale, or supply of lists containing details of trade union members or persons who have participated in trade union activities, where those lists are compiled for the purposes of discrimination in recruitment or treatment of workers. Amended by ERA 2025 s.67 (in force 18 February 2026) to strengthen and extend the prohibition.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 3 is the enabling provision for anti-blacklisting regulations. The substantive prohibition is implemented through the Employment Relations Act 1999 (Blacklists) Regulations 2010 (SI 2010/493).

versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations Act 1999 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: Original enabling provision for blacklist regulations, part of the Fairness at Work programme. The prohibition at subsection (1)(b) was limited to lists compiled 'with a view to being used by employers or employment agencies' — requiring proof of compilation purpose. No subsection (2A) or (3)(za) existed.

ERA 2025 s.67 made four changes: (1) omitted the words 'by employers or employment agencies' from s.3(1)(b), broadening the compilation prohibition; (2) inserted new s.3(2A) creating a separate prohibition on the use and supply of lists containing trade union information for discriminatory purposes — critically, this prohibition does not depend on the list having been compiled with that purpose, closing a loophole in the original provision; (3) inserted s.3(3)(za) extending liability to a person who causes another person to compile, use, sell or supply a prohibited list; (4) added the reference to s.3(2A) in s.3(3)(e) so that dismissal connected with lists caught by the new provision can be addressed in regulations.

refs: Employment Relations Act 1999 (Blacklists) Regulations 2010 (SI 2010/493) — substantive regulations made under this power
TULRCA 1992 s.137 (refusal of employment on grounds related to union membership)
TULRCA 1992 s.146 (detriment short of dismissal for union activities)
ERA 2025 s.67 (amendments to blacklist provisions, in force 18 Feb 2026)
Balfour Beatty Engineering Services Ltd v Unite the Union [2012] EWHC 267 (QB)

leadingCases.name: Smith v Carillion (JM) Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Compensation for blacklisting is not limited to financial loss; injury to feelings is recoverable under the Blacklists Regulations.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the Blacklists Regulations 2010 permit awards for injury to feelings in addition to compensatory awards for financial loss, applying the Vento bands.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the breadth of remedies available under the Blacklists Regulations and established that blacklisting constitutes a form of discrimination warranting injury to feelings awards.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Employment Practices and Data Protection
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Blacklists constitute personal data processing. Compilation and use of blacklists engages UK GDPR obligations including lawful basis, data minimisation, and data subject rights.

commonErrors: Assuming the 2010 Regulations only apply to the construction sector — they apply to all sectors
Failing to recognise that the ERA 2025 amendments (in force 18 Feb 2026) extended the prohibition to cover causing another to compile or use a blacklist
Overlooking data protection obligations arising from the compilation of any list containing trade union membership information

sections.title: Unfair dismissal connected with recognition: interim relief
sections.noLongerHasEffectNote: This section no longer has effect as the textual amendment it made has been incorporated into ERA 1996 ss.128–129. The protection it established — interim relief for recognition-related dismissals — continues in force through TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 para.161(2) as cross-referenced in ERA 1996 s.128(1)(b).
sections.summary: Amended ERA 1996 s.128(1)(b) and s.129(1) to extend interim relief to employees dismissed for reasons connected with trade union recognition under Schedule A1. Although this section itself no longer has effect (the textual amendment having been incorporated), the protection it created remains operative.
sections.text: In sections 128(1)(b) and 129(1) of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (interim relief) after "103" there shall be inserted " or in paragraph 161(2) of Schedule A1 to the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 ".
refs: ERA 1996 s.104A (automatically unfair dismissal for recognition activities)
ERA 1996 s.128–132 (interim relief provisions — amended by this section to cover Schedule A1 dismissals)
TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 para.161(2) (automatically unfair dismissal connected with recognition procedure)

parts.title: Leave for family and domestic reasons

sections.title: Maternity and parental leave
sections.summary: Replaces Part VIII of the Employment Rights Act 1996, establishing the modern framework for maternity leave (ordinary and additional) and introducing parental leave for the first time in UK law. Implements the EU Parental Leave Directive (96/34/EC).
sections.text: For Part VIII of the Employment Rights Act 1996 there shall be substituted the Part set out in Part I of Schedule 4.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 7, together with Schedule 4 Part I, constitutes the statutory foundation for modern maternity and parental leave entitlements in UK law.
refs: ERA 1996 Part VIII (replaced by ERA 1999 s.7 and Schedule 4 Part I)
Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3312) — regulations made under the new Part VIII
EU Parental Leave Directive 96/34/EC (implemented by this provision)
ERA 2025 ss.14–41 (further strengthens maternity/family leave protections — not yet in force)

leadingCases.name: New Southern Railway Ltd v Quinn
leadingCases.principle: An employer is entitled to maintain reasonable contact with an employee on maternity leave. Maternity leave is not a complete severance of the employment relationship — the mutual obligations of trust and confidence continue.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the employer's attempts to contact the employee during maternity leave regarding a proposed restructuring did not constitute a detriment or unfavourable treatment. Reasonable contact is permissible and may be necessary to fulfil the employer's obligations under the Maternity and Parental Leave Regulations, including the duty to notify the employee of suitable alternative vacancies in a redundancy situation.
leadingCases.significance: Established that maternity leave does not create a 'contact embargo'. Reasonable employer-initiated contact is lawful and may be necessary. Now codified in the ACAS guidance on maternity rights. Critical for HR directors managing restructurings where employees are on leave.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Maternity leave and pay
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on the distinction between ordinary and additional maternity leave, notification requirements, keeping in touch days, and the employer's obligations during leave. Includes guidance on reasonable contact during maternity leave (consistent with Quinn).

commonErrors: Confusing the entitlements established by s.7 (the Act) with the detailed conditions in the Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999 which provide the operational detail
Failing to recognise that parental leave (13 weeks per parent per child) was a new right with no UK precedent — it implemented the EU Parental Leave Directive

sections.title: Time off for domestic incidents
sections.summary: Inserts sections 57A and 57B into the Employment Rights Act 1996, creating the right to take reasonable time off work to deal with emergencies involving a dependant (now known as 'time off for dependants'). This is a day-one right requiring no qualifying period.
sections.text: After section 57 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 there shall be inserted the provisions set out in Part II of Schedule 4.
sections.keyPrinciple: The right to time off for dependants is a day-one right — no qualifying period is required. It entitles the employee to reasonable unpaid time off, not a fixed period.
refs: ERA 1996 s.57A (time off for dependants — inserted by this provision)
ERA 1996 s.57B (interpretation of s.57A — inserted by this provision)
ERA 1996 s.99 (automatically unfair dismissal for exercising s.57A rights)
Qua v John Ford Morrison Solicitors [2003] ICR 482 (EAT) — scope of 'reasonable' time off
ERA 2025 s.14 (amendments to dependants' leave — not yet in force)

leadingCases.name: Qua v John Ford Morrison Solicitors
leadingCases.principle: The right to time off for dependants covers the immediate emergency — not the ongoing care needs that follow.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that s.57A entitles the employee to reasonable time off to deal with the immediate emergency or disruption, including making longer-term arrangements, but does not provide an entitlement to extended absence for ongoing care.
leadingCases.significance: Established the scope limitation of dependants' leave — it covers the emergency response, not the sustained care requirement. This remains the leading authority on the temporal scope of s.57A.

sections.title: Consequential amendments
sections.summary: Makes consequential amendments to the Employment Rights Act 1996 flowing from sections 7 and 8, including amendments to the qualifying period for unfair dismissal claims (s.108 ERA 1996) — reducing the qualifying period from two years to one year.
sections.text: The Employment Rights Act 1996 shall be amended as provided in Part III of Schedule 4.
sections.keyPrinciple: The consequential amendments made by Schedule 4 Part III include the highly significant reduction of the unfair dismissal qualifying period from 2 years to 1 year (amending ERA 1996 s.108), which came into force on 1 June 1999.
refs: ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — reduced from 2 years to 1 year by this provision)
ERA 1999 Schedule 4 Part III
Fairness at Work White Paper Cm 3968 (proposed 6-month qualifying period; 1 year was the compromise)

sections.title: Right to be accompanied
sections.summary: Establishes the statutory right for any worker required or invited to attend a disciplinary or grievance hearing to be accompanied by a trade union official or a fellow worker of their choice. The companion may address the hearing, put and sum up the worker's case, and respond on the worker's behalf, but may not answer questions on the worker's behalf.
sections.text:
(1) This section applies where a worker—

(a) is required or invited by his employer to attend a disciplinary or grievance hearing, and

(b) reasonably requests to be accompanied at the hearing.

(2A) Where this section applies, the employer must permit the worker to be accompanied at the hearing by one companion who—

(a) is chosen by the worker; and

(b) is within subsection (3).

(2B) The employer must permit the worker's companion to—

(a) address the hearing in order to do any or all of the following—

(i) put the worker's case;

(ii) sum up that case;

(iii) respond on the worker's behalf to any view expressed at the hearing;

(b) confer with the worker during the hearing.

(2C) Subsection (2B) does not require the employer to permit the worker's companion to—

(a) answer questions on behalf of the worker;

(b) address the hearing if the worker indicates at it that he does not wish his companion to do so; or

(c) use the powers conferred by that subsection in a way that prevents the employer from explaining his case or prevents any other person at the hearing from making his contribution to it.

(3) A person is within this subsection if he is—

(a) employed by a trade union of which he is an official within the meaning of sections 1 and 119 of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992,

(b) an official of a trade union (within that meaning) whom the union has reasonably certified in writing as having experience of, or as having received training in, acting as a worker's companion at disciplinary or grievance hearings, or

(c) another of the employer's workers.

(4) If—

(a) a worker has a right under this section to be accompanied at a hearing,

(b) his chosen companion will not be available at the time proposed for the hearing by the employer, and

(c) the worker proposes an alternative time which satisfies subsection (5),

the employer must postpone the hearing to the time proposed by the worker.

(5) An alternative time must—

(a) be reasonable, and

(b) fall before the end of the period of five working days beginning with the first working day after the day proposed by the employer.

(6) An employer shall permit a worker to take time off during working hours for the purpose of accompanying another of the employer's workers in accordance with a request under subsection (1)(b).

(7) Sections 168(3), (4) and (5), 169 and 171 to 173 of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (time off for carrying out trade union duties) shall apply in relation to subsection (6) above as they apply in relation to section 168(1) of that Act.
sections.keyPrinciple: The right to be accompanied applies to workers (not just employees), covers both disciplinary and grievance hearings, and the companion's role is defined by statute — they may address the hearing and confer with the worker, but may not answer questions on the worker's behalf.

versions.text:
(2) Where this section applies the employer must permit the worker to be accompanied at the hearing by a single companion who—

(a) is chosen by the worker and is within subsection (3),

(b) is to be permitted to address the hearing (but not to answer questions on behalf of the worker), and

(c) is to be permitted to confer with the worker during the hearing.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations Act 1999 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: Original version established the right to be accompanied by a single companion who could address the hearing and confer with the worker, but could not answer questions on the worker's behalf. The companion's permitted role was defined in a single subsection.

The Employment Relations Act 2004 restructured the companion's role across three new subsections (2A, 2B, 2C), replacing the original single subsection (2). The companion's permitted role was significantly expanded: they could now explicitly 'put the worker's case', 'sum up that case', and 'respond on the worker's behalf to any view expressed'. The prohibition on answering questions was retained in the new s.10(2C)(a), and new limitations were added to prevent the companion from overriding the worker's wishes or preventing others from contributing.

versions.text: [Current version — subsection (7) amended by ERA 2025 s.64(7) to update cross-references to TULRCA 1992 s.168.]
versions.policyRationale: ERA 2025 makes consequential amendments to the time off provisions cross-referenced in s.10(7), updating references to TULRCA 1992 s.168 to reflect the restructured right to paid time off for union duties.

refs: ERA 1999 s.11 (complaint to employment tribunal for breach of s.10)
ERA 1999 s.12 (detriment and dismissal for exercising s.10 rights)
ERA 1999 s.13 (interpretation — definition of 'worker', 'disciplinary hearing', 'grievance hearing')
ERA 1999 s.14 (contracting out and conciliation)
ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015) — para 13–17
TULRCA 1992 s.168–173 (time off for trade union duties — applied by s.10(7))
ERA 2004 s.37 (amendments to companion's role)
ERA 2025 s.64(7) (consequential amendment to s.10(7))
Toal v GB Oils Ltd [2013] UKEAT/0569/12

leadingCases.name: Toal v GB Oils Ltd
leadingCases.principle: The reasonableness requirement in s.10(1)(b) applies to the request to be accompanied, not to the identity of the chosen companion. The employer cannot refuse a companion who falls within s.10(3), regardless of practical inconvenience.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that s.10(2A)(a) gives the worker the right to choose their companion, subject only to the requirement that the companion falls within one of the three categories in s.10(3) — a trade union official, a certified trade union representative, or a fellow worker. The word 'reasonably' in s.10(1)(b) qualifies the request to be accompanied, not the choice of who should accompany the worker. An employer who refused to allow a certified Unite official to attend as companion was in breach of s.10, even though the workers subsequently arranged for a different colleague to attend instead.
leadingCases.significance: The leading EAT authority on companion choice under s.10. Rejected the prevailing interpretation (derived from ACAS Code paragraph 15) that the employer could refuse a companion on grounds of practical inconvenience or geographical remoteness. Led directly to the revision of the ACAS Code of Practice. Compensation was limited to a nominal sum where the workers suffered no actual loss or detriment from the breach.

leadingCases.name: London Underground Ltd v Ferenc-Batchelor
leadingCases.principle: The right to be accompanied arises when the worker 'reasonably requests' it — the reasonableness is assessed objectively, not by reference to the employer's view.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the question of whether a request to be accompanied is reasonable is an objective question for the tribunal, and the employer cannot unilaterally determine that a request is unreasonable.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the objective test for reasonable requests under s.10(1)(b) and that the employer's subjective assessment of reasonableness is not determinative.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Paragraphs 13–17 of the ACAS Code address the right to be accompanied. The Code is admissible in evidence in employment tribunal proceedings and a failure to follow it may result in an uplift of up to 25% to any award.

commonErrors: Restricting the right to employees only — s.10 applies to workers, which is a broader category including agency workers and home workers (s.13(1))
Denying accompaniment at grievance hearings — the right applies to both disciplinary AND grievance hearings as defined in s.13(4) and (5)
Preventing the companion from addressing the hearing or conferring with the worker — s.10(2B) expressly permits both, including putting the worker's case and summing up
Refusing to reschedule when the chosen companion is unavailable — s.10(4) requires postponement for up to 5 working days if the worker proposes a reasonable alternative time

Attempting to restrict the worker's choice of companion to a particular individual — Toal v GB Oils [2013] IRLR 696 confirmed the employer cannot refuse any companion who falls within the s.10(3) categories, regardless of practical inconvenience

Applying the right to investigation meetings — the ACAS Code of Practice confirms there is no statutory right to be accompanied at investigatory meetings; s.10 applies only to hearings that could result in formal action (s.13(4)) or concern a duty owed by the employer (s.13(5)). However, if an investigatory meeting evolves into a disciplinary hearing, the right is engaged

Permitting a solicitor, legal representative, or family member as a companion — s.10(3) limits the companion to three categories only: a trade union official, a certified trade union representative, or a fellow worker. There is no statutory right to legal representation at internal disciplinary or grievance hearings (though Article 6 ECHR may engage in limited circumstances where professional standing is at stake)

Failing to inform the worker of their right to be accompanied in the invitation letter — while not a strict statutory requirement, the ACAS Code expects this and failure to do so may lead to an uplift in compensation

sections.title: Complaint to employment tribunal
sections.summary: Provides the enforcement mechanism for s.10 — a worker denied the right to be accompanied may present a complaint to an employment tribunal within three months. If the complaint is upheld, the tribunal may order compensation of up to two weeks' pay.
sections.text:
(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that his employer has failed, or threatened to fail, to comply with section 10(2A), (2B) or (4).

(2) A tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this section in relation to a failure or threat unless it is presented—

(a) before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date of the failure or threat, or

(b) within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be presented before the end of that period of three months.

(3) Where a tribunal finds that a complaint under this section is well-founded it shall order the employer to pay compensation to the worker of an amount not exceeding two weeks' pay.

(4) Chapter II of Part XIV of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (calculation of a week's pay) shall apply for the purposes of subsection (3); and in applying that Chapter the calculation date shall be taken to be—

(a) in the case of a claim which is made in the course of a claim for unfair dismissal, the date on which the employer's notice of dismissal was given or, if there was no notice, the effective date of termination, and

(b) in any other case, the date on which the relevant hearing took place (or was to have taken place).
refs: ERA 1999 s.10 (right to be accompanied — the substantive right enforced by this section)
ERA 1996 Part XIV Chapter II (calculation of a week's pay)
ERA 2025 Sch.12 para.8 (amendments to s.11(2) — not yet in force)

pendingAmendment.effect: Substitutes wording in s.11(2)(a) and (b) to align time limit provisions with the new ERA 2025 time limit framework.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: To be confirmed by commencement order
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — procedural change to time limits for s.10 complaints.

sections.title: Detriment and dismissal
sections.summary: Protects workers from suffering detriment, and employees from unfair dismissal, for exercising or seeking to exercise the right to be accompanied under s.10 or for accompanying another worker. Dismissal for this reason is automatically unfair.
sections.text:
(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by his employer done on the ground that the worker—

(a) exercised or sought to exercise the right under section 10(2A), (2B) or (4), or

(b) accompanied or sought to accompany another worker (whether of the same employer or not) pursuant to a request under that section.

(3) Section 48 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 shall apply in relation to contraventions of subsection (1) above as it applies in relation to contraventions of certain sections of that Act.

(4) A worker who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of Part X of the Employment Rights Act 1996 as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that he—

(a) exercised or sought to exercise the right under section 10(2A), (2B) or (4), or

(b) accompanied or sought to accompany another worker (whether of the same employer or not) pursuant to a request under that section.

(5) Sections 108 and 109 of that Act (qualifying period of employment and upper age limit) shall not apply in relation to subsection (4) above.

(6) Sections 128 to 132 of that Act (interim relief) shall apply in relation to dismissal for the reason specified in subsection (4)(a) or (b) above as they apply in relation to dismissal for a reason specified in section 128(1)(b) of that Act.

(7) In the application of Chapter II of Part X of that Act in relation to subsection (4) above, a reference to an employee shall be taken as a reference to a worker.
sections.keyPrinciple: Dismissal for exercising the right to be accompanied is automatically unfair — no qualifying period of employment is required. Interim relief is available.
refs: ERA 1999 s.10 (the substantive right protected by this section)
ERA 1996 s.48 (enforcement of detriment claims)
ERA 1996 Part X (unfair dismissal — applied to workers by s.12(7))
ERA 1996 s.108 (qualifying period — disapplied by s.12(5))
ERA 1996 s.128–132 (interim relief — applied by s.12(6))
commonErrors: Not recognising that dismissal for exercising s.10 rights is AUTOMATICALLY unfair — no qualifying period applies (s.12(5) disapplies s.108). This means even a day-one employee dismissed for requesting accompaniment can claim unfair dismissal

Overlooking that interim relief is available (s.12(6)) — the employee can apply within 7 days of the effective date of termination for an order preserving their employment pending the full tribunal hearing

Failing to recognise that the protection extends to accompanying ANOTHER worker — s.12(4)(b) protects workers who accompany or seek to accompany colleagues, not just workers who exercise their own right

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.summary: Defines key terms for sections 10–15 including 'worker' (which uses a broader definition than 'employee'), 'disciplinary hearing' (a hearing which could result in a formal warning, other action, or confirmation of a warning or action), and 'grievance hearing'.
sections.text:
(1) In sections 10 to 12 and this section "worker" means an individual who is—

(a) a worker within the meaning of section 230(3) of the Employment Rights Act 1996,

(b) an agency worker,

(c) a home worker, or

(d) a person in Crown employment within the meaning of section 191 of that Act, other than a member of the naval, military, air or reserve forces of the Crown.

(4) For the purposes of section 10 a disciplinary hearing is a hearing which could result in—

(a) the administration of a formal warning to a worker by his employer,

(b) the taking of some other action in respect of a worker by his employer, or

(c) the confirmation of a warning issued or some other action taken.

(5) For the purposes of section 10 a grievance hearing is a hearing which concerns the performance of a duty by an employer in relation to a worker.
sections.keyPrinciple: The definition of 'worker' for the right to be accompanied is intentionally broader than the ERA 1996 definition of 'employee' — it includes agency workers, home workers, and Crown employees.
refs: ERA 1996 s.230(3) (definition of 'worker')
ERA 1999 s.10–12 (provisions to which these definitions apply)
commonErrors: Not recognising the breadth of the 'worker' definition in s.13(1) — it extends beyond the ERA 1996 s.230(3) definition to explicitly include agency workers and home workers. This means agency workers have the right to be accompanied even though they may not have unfair dismissal rights

Misunderstanding what constitutes a 'disciplinary hearing' under s.13(4) — it is any hearing that COULD result in a formal warning or other action. The test is prospective: if there is a possibility of formal action, the right is engaged. It does not matter that the employer intends the meeting to be informal

Misunderstanding the definition of 'grievance hearing' in s.13(5) — it must concern 'the performance of a duty by an employer in relation to a worker'. A complaint about a purely discretionary matter (e.g., the amount of a bonus that is not contractually guaranteed) may not engage the right

sections.title: Unfair dismissal of striking workers
sections.summary: Inserts new protections against the dismissal of employees taking part in lawfully organised official industrial action, through amendments to TULRCA 1992 (inserting s.238A). An employee dismissed during protected industrial action within the first 12 weeks is automatically unfairly dismissed.
sections.text: Schedule 5 (which amends the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 and the Employment Rights Act 1996 in relation to the dismissal of employees taking part in industrial action) shall have effect.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 16 and Schedule 5 create protected period unfair dismissal — dismissal during lawfully organised official industrial action within the first 12 weeks (the 'protected period') is automatically unfair.
refs: TULRCA 1992 s.238A (protected industrial action — inserted by ERA 1999 Schedule 5)
TULRCA 1992 s.238 (dismissals in connection with industrial action — amended)
TULRCA 1992 s.239 (supplementary provisions — amended)
ERA 1996 s.105 (unfair selection for redundancy — consequentially amended)
ERA 1999 Schedule 5
commonErrors: Assuming that dismissing striking workers is always lawful — s.238A provides that dismissal during the first 12 weeks of lawfully organised official industrial action is automatically unfair. After 12 weeks, the dismissal may still be unfair if the employer has not taken reasonable procedural steps to resolve the dispute

Not recognising the distinction between official and unofficial industrial action — the s.238A protections only apply to official industrial action. Dismissal of employees taking part in unofficial action is governed by TULRCA 1992 s.237, which largely removes the right to claim unfair dismissal

Selectively dismissing some strikers but not others — TULRCA 1992 s.238 provides that where all employees taking industrial action at an establishment are dismissed, none can claim unfair dismissal. But if only some are dismissed, or some are re-engaged within 3 months, those dismissed can claim

sections.title: Power to confer rights on individuals
sections.summary: Provides the Secretary of State with a power to make regulations extending employment protection rights to categories of individuals who may not fall within the existing statutory definitions of 'employee' or 'worker'. This is a significant enabling provision for future extension of employment rights.
sections.text:
(1) This section applies to any right conferred on individuals by or under any of the following—

(a) the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992;

(b) the Employment Rights Act 1996;

(c) this Act; or

(d) any instrument made under an Act referred to in paragraphs (a) to (c).

(2) The Secretary of State may by order make provision which has the effect of conferring any such right on individuals who are of a specified description.

(3) The reference in subsection (2) to individuals includes a reference to individuals who are not combatants.

(4) An order under this section—

(a) shall be made by statutory instrument, and

(b) may include such consequential, incidental or supplementary provision as the Secretary of State thinks fit.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 23 is a broad enabling power to extend employment rights by statutory instrument, without requiring primary legislation. It has not been used to extend rights to agency workers — the Agency Workers Regulations 2010 were instead made under the European Communities Act 1972.
refs: TULRCA 1992 (rights which may be extended under s.23)
ERA 1996 (rights which may be extended under s.23)
Agency Workers Regulations 2010 (SI 2010/93) — made under a different power despite s.23 being available
commonErrors: Assuming that because someone does not meet the definition of 'employee' they have no employment rights — s.23 gives the Secretary of State broad power to extend rights to additional categories of individuals by order, and many rights already apply to the broader 'worker' category

Not monitoring statutory instruments made under s.23 — rights can be extended without primary legislation, meaning the scope of employment protection can change through secondary legislation that may not attract the same public attention as an Act of Parliament

sections.title: Indexation of amounts, &c.
sections.summary: Provides the Secretary of State with power to increase by order various financial limits in employment legislation in line with the retail prices index, including the compensatory award cap for unfair dismissal, the basic award, and a week's pay. Multiple amendments by ERA 2025 Schedule 3 are pending commencement.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 34 is the statutory mechanism by which the compensatory award cap, basic award minimum, a week's pay limit, guarantee pay, and insolvency limits are annually uprated by reference to the retail prices index. The current limits (from April 2024): compensatory award cap £115,115; a week's pay £643; basic award minimum £8,533.

versions.text: [Original s.34(1) listed paragraphs (a)-(e) and (f)-(g) only — no (za), (zb), or (ea). Section 34(4) set the initial compensatory award cap at £50,000.]
versions.amendedBy: Employment Relations Act 1999 as originally enacted
versions.policyRationale: Original indexation provision set the compensatory award cap at £50,000 (up from the previous limit) and established the RPI-linked annual uprating mechanism.

versions.text: [s.34(1)(ea) and revised (f) substituted by ERA 2004 Sch.1 para.42(2), adding TULRCA s.145E(3) (unlawful inducements award) and s.156(1) (minimum basic award for union-related dismissal). s.34(1)(g) reference updated to s.176(6A) by ERA 2004 Sch.1 para.42(3).]
versions.policyRationale: ERA 2004 added new sums to the indexation mechanism — the unlawful inducements award (new TULRCA s.145E) and the union-related minimum basic award (TULRCA s.156) — ensuring these new limits would also be annually uprated.

versions.text: [s.34(2) words omitted and replaced by ERRA 2013 s.22(2), changing the timing mechanism to 'with effect from the following 6th April'. s.34(3) rounding rule updated. s.34(4A) and (4B) inserted by ERRA 2013 s.15(10), excluding the Simmons v Castle element from RPI uprating.]
versions.amendedBy: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 ss.15 and 22
versions.policyRationale: ERRA 2013 restructured the compensatory award cap to a dual mechanism: (1) the RPI-linked annual uprating under s.34; or (2) a cap expressed as a multiple of the employee's annual salary (currently 52 weeks' pay) whichever is lower — introduced by ERRA 2013 s.15. The s.34(4A)/(4B) provisions prevent double-counting.

versions.text: [Current version — s.34(1)(za) and (zb) inserted by Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023 s.12(4)(a), adding tips compensation and information compensation to the indexation mechanism. s.34(1)(a) cross-reference updated.]
versions.policyRationale: The Tips Act 2023 introduced new compensation limits for tips-related complaints (ERA 1996 ss.27M and 27O). Section 34 was amended to bring these new limits within the annual RPI-linked uprating mechanism, ensuring they keep pace with inflation.

refs: ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award cap — uprated under s.34)
ERA 1996 s.227 (a week's pay limit — uprated under s.34)
ERA 2025 Sch.3 para.2 and para.5 (amendments to s.34 — not yet in force)
Employment Rights (Increase of Limits) Order 2024 (SI 2024/182) — most recent uprating order

pendingAmendment.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 Sch.3 paras.2 and 5
pendingAmendment.effect: Omits s.34(1)(c) and s.34(4)-(4B), and confers new power to amend s.34 by regulations. Part of the broader ERA 2025 restructuring of financial limits in employment law.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Not yet in force as at March 2026
pendingAmendment.anticipatedDate: To be confirmed by commencement order
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: HIGH — affects the mechanism by which all key employment law financial limits are uprated.

currentLimits.compensatoryAwardCap: £115,115 (or 52 weeks' gross pay if lower — dual cap from ERRA 2013 s.15)
currentLimits.minimumBasicAward: £8,533 (union-related and health and safety dismissals only)
currentLimits.nextUprating: April 2025 — based on September 2024 RPI figures

commonErrors: Not knowing that the compensatory award has a dual cap post-ERRA 2013 — the cap is the LOWER of the RPI-linked annual limit (currently £115,115) or 52 weeks' gross pay. For employees earning less than approximately £2,214 per week, the 52-week multiple is the binding cap

Assuming the uprating is discretionary — s.34(2) imposes a mandatory duty on the Secretary of State to make an uprating order each year based on the September RPI movement. The uprating is automatic, not optional

Confusing the statutory redundancy payment calculation (which uses the week's pay cap of £643) with the compensatory award for unfair dismissal (which uses a separate cap of £115,115). The two caps serve different purposes and are calculated differently
Overlooking that certain categories of unfair dismissal have NO compensatory award cap — whistleblowing (s.103A) and health and safety (s.100) dismissals attract uncapped compensation
Failing to account for the Tips Act 2023 additions — s.34(1)(za) and (zb) now bring tips compensation limits within the annual uprating mechanism (in force from October 2024)

parts.title: Schedule A1 — Collective Bargaining: Recognition (Key Provisions)

sections.title: Request for recognition and employer response
sections.summary: The statutory recognition procedure begins with the trade union making a written request to the employer. The request must identify the union, the proposed bargaining unit, and state that it is made under Schedule A1. The employer must respond within 10 working days — agreeing to recognise, requesting negotiations about the bargaining unit, or rejecting the request. If the employer does not respond or rejects the request, the union may apply to the Central Arbitration Committee (CAC).
sections.text:
Paragraph 4 of Schedule A1 to TULRCA 1992 (inserted by ERA 1999 Schedule 1) provides: The request is not valid unless it is received by the employer. The request is not valid unless the union (or each of the unions) has a certificate under section 6 that it is independent. The request is not valid unless the employer, taken with any associated employer or employers, employs—

(a) at least 21 workers on the day the employer receives the request, or

(b) an average of at least 21 workers in the 13 weeks ending with that day.
sections.keyPrinciple: The statutory recognition procedure applies only to employers with 21 or more workers. The threshold is 'workers' not 'employees' — the broader definition. Once the request is made, the employer has strict time limits for responding.
refs: TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 paras.1–11 (request and response)
TULRCA 1992 s.6 (certificate of independence)
ERA 1999 s.1 (inserts Schedule A1 into TULRCA 1992)
R (Boots Management Services Ltd) v CAC [2014] EWHC 2930 (Admin) — scope of bargaining unit determination
commonErrors: Counting only employees for the 21-worker threshold — the test uses the broader 'worker' definition
Failing to respond to the union's request within 10 working days — this can result in the CAC accepting the application
Not recognising that associated employers' workers are counted towards the 21-worker threshold

sections.title: CAC determination and recognition ballots
sections.summary: Where the parties cannot agree on the bargaining unit, the CAC determines the appropriate unit. The CAC then decides whether to order recognition without a ballot (where a majority of workers in the unit are union members) or to hold a secret ballot. The union is recognised if a majority of those voting, and at least 40% of the workers in the bargaining unit, vote in favour of recognition.
sections.text:
Paragraph 22(2) of Schedule A1 provides: The CAC must decide whether the proposed bargaining unit is appropriate. Paragraph 22(3): If the CAC decides that the proposed bargaining unit is not appropriate, it must decide a bargaining unit which is appropriate. Paragraph 22(4): In deciding whether the proposed bargaining unit is appropriate and in deciding an appropriate bargaining unit, the CAC must take into account the need for the unit to be compatible with effective management.

Paragraph 29(2) provides: The union is recognised as entitled to conduct collective bargaining on behalf of the group of workers constituting the bargaining unit if (a) the CAC is satisfied that a majority of the workers constituting the bargaining unit are members of the union, and (b) the CAC does not consider that a ballot should be held.
sections.keyPrinciple: Recognition requires either: (a) CAC declaration without ballot where a majority of workers in the unit are union members and no ballot is deemed necessary; or (b) a ballot in which a majority of those voting, and at least 40% of all workers in the unit, vote in favour. The dual threshold (majority voting + 40% of unit) protects against low-turnout results.
refs: TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 paras.19–29
Fullarton Computer Industries Ltd v CAC [2001] IRLR 752 — CAC approach to bargaining unit determination
R (Cable & Wireless Services UK Ltd) v CAC [2008] EWHC 115 (Admin) — scope of judicial review of CAC decisions
commonErrors: Assuming the employer can choose the bargaining unit — the CAC determines the appropriate unit, taking into account effective management compatibility, existing national/local bargaining arrangements, and the need to avoid small fragmented units. The employer's preferred unit may be rejected

Not recognising the dual threshold for ballot success — the union must achieve BOTH a majority of those voting AND at least 40% of all workers in the bargaining unit. A 51% vote in favour on a 50% turnout (25.5% of the unit) would fail the 40% threshold

Failing to understand that the CAC can declare recognition WITHOUT a ballot where a majority of workers in the unit are union members — this 'automatic recognition' route means the employer may face recognition without ever going to a ballot

sections.title: Employer-initiated derecognition
sections.summary: An employer may seek to end statutory recognition by giving written notice to the union if the employer believes the original bargaining unit is no longer appropriate. Where a recognised union's membership falls below 50% of the bargaining unit, the employer may request a derecognition ballot. The CAC may also order a derecognition ballot where the employer can demonstrate that a majority of workers in the bargaining unit no longer support recognition.
sections.text:
Paragraph 99 of Schedule A1 provides: This Part of this Schedule applies if the CAC has issued a declaration that a union is (or unions are) recognised as entitled to conduct collective bargaining on behalf of a bargaining unit.

Paragraph 104(1) provides: The employer may apply to the CAC to have the bargaining arrangements ended on the grounds that fewer than half the workers constituting the bargaining unit are members of the union (or of any of the unions).
sections.keyPrinciple: Derecognition is not unilateral — the employer must follow the Schedule A1 procedure. The employer cannot simply withdraw recognition. A ballot may be required, and the same 40% threshold applies in reverse.
refs: TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 paras.99–133 (Part IV — derecognition)
TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 paras.134–148 (Part V — derecognition where employer is small)
R (Boots Management Services Ltd) v CAC [2014] EWHC 2930 (Admin)
commonErrors: Assuming the employer can simply withdraw recognition — statutory recognition can only be ended through the Schedule A1 procedure or by mutual agreement
Not recognising that the 3-year moratorium applies after initial recognition — no derecognition application may be made within 3 years of the original declaration
Confusing voluntary recognition (can be withdrawn unilaterally unless contractually binding) with statutory recognition (can only be ended via Schedule A1)

sections.title: Protection from detriment and dismissal connected with recognition
sections.summary: Workers have the right not to suffer detriment, and employees the right not to be unfairly dismissed, for exercising rights or participating in processes under the recognition procedure. Dismissal for a recognition-related reason is automatically unfair — no qualifying period is required. Interim relief is available (as extended by ERA 1999 s.6).
sections.text:
Paragraph 161(1) of Schedule A1 provides: An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded for the purposes of Part X of the Employment Rights Act 1996 as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is one specified in paragraph 161(2).

Paragraph 161(2) specifies: that the worker acted with a view to obtaining or preventing recognition, or that the worker indicated support for or opposition to recognition, or that the worker voted or proposed to vote in a recognition ballot.
sections.keyPrinciple: Protection from dismissal connected with the recognition process is absolute — no qualifying period is required, and interim relief is available. This ensures that employers cannot defeat recognition campaigns through dismissals.
refs: TULRCA 1992 Schedule A1 paras.156–165 (Part VI — protection)
ERA 1996 s.128–132 (interim relief — extended to cover para.161(2) dismissals by ERA 1999 s.6)
ERA 1996 Part X (unfair dismissal — applied by para.161)
commonErrors: Dismissing an employee who has indicated support for recognition during a recognition campaign — this is automatically unfair under para.161(2) regardless of the employee's length of service

Not recognising that interim relief is available within 7 days of the effective date of termination — the employee can apply for an order preserving their employment pending the full hearing, and the employer faces significant cost exposure if the application succeeds

Overlooking that the protection covers not just voting in a recognition ballot but also indicating support for or opposition to recognition, and acting with a view to obtaining or preventing recognition — the scope is deliberately broad

sections.title: Part-time work: discrimination
sections.summary: Provides the Secretary of State with power to make regulations to ensure that part-time workers are not treated less favourably than comparable full-time workers, unless the less favourable treatment can be objectively justified. This enabling provision led to the Part-Time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000 (SI 2000/1551), implementing the EU Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC.
sections.keyPrinciple: Section 19 is the enabling provision for the Part-Time Workers Regulations 2000. The substantive protections — including the right to equal treatment in pay, contractual terms, training, and access to occupational pension schemes — are found in the Regulations, not in the Act itself.
refs: Part-Time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000 (SI 2000/1551) — the substantive regulations
EU Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC (implemented by the Regulations)
ERA 1999 s.20 (code of practice on part-time work)
ERA 1999 s.21 (code of practice: supplemental provisions)
Matthews v Kent and Medway Towns Fire Authority [2006] ICR 365 (UKHL) — comparator test for part-time workers

leadingCases.name: Matthews v Kent and Medway Towns Fire Authority
leadingCases.principle: A part-time worker is employed under the same type of contract as a full-time worker where, broadly, both do the same or broadly similar work. Differences in the range or type of duties performed do not necessarily prevent a valid comparison.
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that retained (part-time) firefighters could compare themselves with full-time firefighters for the purposes of the Part-Time Workers Regulations, even though there were differences in the range of duties each performed. The comparison is between the 'type of contract' not the precise duties.
leadingCases.significance: The leading UKHL authority on the comparator test under the Part-Time Workers Regulations. Broadened the scope of comparison by holding that the test focuses on the type of contract, not the precise match of duties.
